title: 1857 Rebellion and Administrative Reorganisation
focus: Covers the role of Begum Hazrat Mahal in the 1857 revolt and the subsequent administrative merger into the United Provinces.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 057]
The Indian Rebellion of 1857
01 Young Ruler Crowned
In the subsequent Indian Rebellion of 1857, Wajid Ali Shah's 14-year-old son Birjis Qadra, whose mother was Begum Hazrat Mahal, was crowned ruler.
02 British Response
English officers Sir Henry Havelock and Sir James Outram led initial forces, followed by a stronger force under Sir Colin Campbell.
03 Rebellion's Defeat
Following the rebellion's defeat, Begum Hazrat Mahal and other rebel leaders sought asylum in Nepal.
04 Return to British Rule
With the 1857 rebellion over, Oudh returned to British governance under a chief commissioner.

[Page 058]
Administrative Reorganisation
* 1877: Offices of lieutenant-governor of the North-Western Provinces and chief commissioner of Oudh were combined.
* 1901: Lucknow, with a population of 264,049, was merged into the newly formed United Provinces of Agra and Oudh.
* 1902: Title of chief commissioner was dropped with the formation of the United Provinces of Agra and Oudh.
evidenceBullets: In the subsequent Indian Rebellion of 1857, Wajid Ali Shah's 14-year-old son Birjis Qadra, whose mother was Begum Hazrat Mahal, was crowned ruler.
English officers Sir Henry Havelock and Sir James Outram led initial forces, followed by a stronger force under Sir Colin Campbell.
